title: Best Drywall Cutting Tools of 2026: Folds That Lock & Cut Clean
excerpt: Which drywall cutting tools lock tight for safe carry, and which cordless cutters survive rough use without breaking bits—for home and pro jobs.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Which drywall cutting tools lock tight for safe carry, and which cordless cutters survive rough use without breaking bits—for home and pro jobs.
intro:
Most drywall jab saws look like they came off the same assembly line, but the difference between a clean cut and a trip to the ER often comes down to whether a folding lock actually stays locked. In the powered world, rotary cut-out tools promise fast electrical box holes, yet the industry’s dirty secret is that chucks routinely jam and snap bits—a frustration that many buyers discover only after the first broken bit is stuck inside.

The right tool for you depends on your storage needs, the kind of drywall you’re cutting, and whether you’re working in an occupied home where dust control matters. Our recommendations focus on models that lock solid, cut straight, and survive real job-site abuse, so you can finish the hole and move on without tool drama.

products.title: Goldblatt Folding Drywall Saw, Jab Saw, Hand Saws with Soft Grip Handle, Sheetrock Saws for Wallboard, Drywall, Plywood and PVC
products.label: Goldblatt Folding Jab Saw
products.oneLiner: Folding jab saw with triple-ground teeth that locks solid and glides through drywall.
pros: Bi-metal teeth slice through drywall, plywood, and PVC with minimal effort. — 112 mentions, 91% positive — cuts like butter
Folding mechanism locks positively, preventing blade deployment in tool bags. — 63 mentions, 95% positive
Sharp tip punches through without a pilot hole, saving setup time. — 78 mentions, 87% positive
cons: Folding hinge may introduce slight flex compared to a non-folding fixed-blade saw.
Manual operation is slower than a rotary cut-out tool for repeated outlet openings.
products.verdict: A sharp bi-metal blade, secure folding lock, and budget-friendly price make this the manual jab saw that makes sense for anyone who cuts drywall, plywood, or PVC by hand.
products.review:
The Goldblatt folding jab saw’s 8 TPI bi-metal blade rips through drywall, plywood, and PVC with a smooth, aggressive cut that feels more like a premium tool than a budget one. It holds its edge through repeated jobs, cutting with noticeably less effort than flimsy discount saws. The anti-slip soft grip handle keeps your hand comfortable during overhead or awkward-angle cuts, and the sharp tip punches through sheetrock without a pilot hole. When you fold the blade into the handle, the lock button securely stows it—no accidental openings in a tool bag.

This saw is ideal for DIYers and pros who need a portable, safe-storing backup for small to medium drywall tasks. Because it’s a manual jab saw, repetitive plunge cuts for rows of outlet boxes will be slower than with a rotary cut-out tool, so it’s best paired with a powered option on large jobs. For homeowners, remodelers, and anyone wanting a one-saw solution that won’t tear up a tool bag, it’s a sharp, go-to choice.

products.title: Klein Tools 31737 Folding Jab Saw / Drywall Hand Saw with Lockback at 180 and 125 Degrees and Tether Hole
products.label: Klein 31737 Folding Jab Saw
products.oneLiner: Dual-angle locking saw with tether hole that cuts at 125° and 180°—pouch-friendly.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Press the lock release until you feel a firm click before cutting — this prevents unexpected fold-aways.
pros: Premium construction withstands frequent job-site use. — 109 mentions, 98% positive
Dual-angle lock at 125° and 180° for tight-space versatility. — 57 mentions, 93% positive
Folding design keeps blade protected and won't tear tool pouches. — 30 mentions, 100% positive
cons: Lock may not always catch securely, with occasional unintended release — a quick pre-cut check avoids surprises. — 21 mentions, 33% negative — some users report the lock releasing unintentionally
products.verdict: For tool-bag durability and dual-angle cutting, this Klein is a solid companion — just develop the habit of a quick lock check before you start.
products.review:
The Klein 31737 locks securely at both 125° and 180°, giving you a straight jab-saw position and an angled reach for flush cuts or tight corners. The carbon steel blade arrives sharp and handles repeated cuts, while the cushioned handle-end and tether hole let you clip it into a pouch without worry — the folding design protects your bag and the blade.

Electricians and pros who need safe, two-angle storage will appreciate the build. The lock can occasionally feel loose; taking a second to confirm engagement prevents unexpected release.

💡 💡 Tip: Press the lock release until you feel a firm click before cutting — this prevents unexpected fold-aways.

products.title: DEWALT Rotary Saw with 1/8-Inch and 1/4-Inch Collets, 5-Amp, Corded (DW660)
products.label: DEWALT DW660 Rotary Saw
products.oneLiner: Corded rotary saw spinning at 30,000 RPM for fast box cutouts on big jobs.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Gently tighten bits and avoid forcing stuck collets; an occasional drop of oil on the mechanism helps prevent jams.
pros: Rips through drywall at 30,000 RPM for rapid cutouts — 92 mentions, 89% positive
Mid-range price delivers corded power without a premium tag — 85 mentions, 82% positive
Clean, minimal-resistance cuts through drywall even in thick sections — 99 mentions, 82% positive
cons: Chuck may seize and snap bits, demanding extra care during bit changes — Durability: 99 mentions, 65% negative — many reports of bits snapping inside, chuck seizing, and tool failure after limited use
products.verdict: A corded powerhouse for big drywall projects, but the chuck’s fragility demands a light touch — a calculated tradeoff for patient DIYers.
products.review:
Unlike battery-dependent cut-out tools, the DW660 runs corded for non-stop 30,000 RPM through sheet after sheet of drywall. That corded hustle means you won’t slow down mid-job, and the mid-range price keeps it accessible for serious DIY renovations.

Homeowners and remodelers tackling large drywall cutouts will appreciate the relentless speed, as long as they can plug in. Those who can treat the chuck carefully will get the most out of it; daily pros may find the downtime too costly.

💡 💡 Tip: Gently tighten bits and avoid forcing stuck collets; an occasional drop of oil on the mechanism helps prevent jams.

products.title: Makita XDS01Z 18V LXT Lithium-Ion Cordless Cut-Out Saw, Tool Only
products.label: Makita XDS01Z Cut‑Out Saw
products.oneLiner: Virtually dust-free reciprocating saw with clear dust box—perfect for finished homes.
pros: Near-zero dust output protects air quality and finishes during occupied remodels. — 70 mentions, 91% positive
Clean, fast cuts minimize debris and reduce post-cut cleanup. — 60 mentions, 90% positive and 43 mentions, 98% positive for performance
Lightweight design gives easy control, even for first-time cut-out tool users. — 28 mentions, 86% positive
cons: Proprietary blades are expensive, so consumable cost can add up quickly. — 52 mentions, 56% negative
products.verdict: A unique dust-free solution that makes sense for professionals cutting into lived-in homes daily — but the blade expense means it is overkill for occasional DIY projects.
products.review: Near-zero dust output sets the Makita apart from every other drywall cut-out tool. A clear dust box and optional vacuum hookup trap debris before it becomes airborne, so cutting into a painted living room doesn’t require tenting off the whole floor. Lightweight and easy to control, it makes clean, fast cutouts achievable even for crew members who rarely pick up a rotary tool. This is the go-to for remodeling contractors working in occupied, furnished homes where dust control is non-negotiable. The blade system is proprietary and replacement blades are expensive, so the tool is a premium investment; occasional drywall holes won’t justify the ongoing consumable cost. It runs on Makita 18V LXT batteries (sold separately), fitting naturally into an existing Makita cordless fleet.

products.title: MILWAUKEE'S 2627-20 M18 18-Volt Lithium-Ion Cordless Cut Out Tool Bare Tool
products.label: Milwaukee M18 Cut‑Out Tool
products.oneLiner: Lightweight M18 cut-out tool with 28,000 RPM and a trouble-free collet.
pros: Consistently clean cuts and dependable drywall performance — 33 mentions, 97% positive
Quick cutting with a minimal learning curve — 27 mentions, 100% positive
Lightweight 1.9‑lb design eases overhead work — 6 mentions, 100% positive
cons: Bare tool only — requires an M18 battery and charger, which adds cost for new buyers
products.verdict: For M18 platform users, this lightweight cut‑out tool delivers fast drywall cuts and avoids the collet headaches that shadow some cordless competitors. New buyers should only consider it when ready to invest in the battery ecosystem.
products.review:
Weighing only 1.9 pounds, the Milwaukee M18 cut‑out tool spins at 28,000 RPM for quick drywall cutouts, and its collet historically holds bits securely without the jamming that frustrates users of some other cordless models. Where the slimmer brushless option demands careful tightening to keep bits from slipping, this tool stays stable with less fuss.

It is a natural fit for trade professionals already invested in the M18 battery platform, especially those doing repetitive overhead cutouts where the light weight reduces arm fatigue. New buyers without M18 batteries will face a higher total cost because this is a bare tool only — a charger and battery are added expenses.

products.title: IRWIN Tools Standard Drywall/Jab Saw (2014102)
products.label: IRWIN Standard Jab Saw
products.oneLiner: Ultra-affordable fixed jab saw with bi-ground teeth; perfect for occasional holes.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Drill a small pilot hole before plunging to avoid blade wobble and get a perfectly straight start.
pros: Exceptional value—cuts like a much costlier saw for pocket change. — 78 mentions, 99% positive
Hardwood handle and rigid blade hold up to light-duty use without degradation. — 70 mentions, 93% positive
Clean, fast drywall cutting; bi-ground teeth don't clog. — 52 mentions, 94% positive
cons: Blade flex can be noticeable when starting a plunge without a pilot hole. — 52 mentions on cut quality, 6% negative — several reviews note flex during jabbing
products.verdict: When your drywall work is sporadic and you value simplicity over versatility, this IRWIN is a no-brainer at its cost—just keep a drill bit handy for starting.
products.review:
Where folding jab saws rely on a locking hinge that can loosen over time, the IRWIN's fixed blade eliminates that failure point entirely. The hardened 6-inch, 7 TPI blade bites through drywall quickly, and the hardwood handle feels solid despite the budget price.

A pilot hole or careful angled entry is all it takes to start cleanly—handy for occasional electrical box cutouts or small drywall repairs. It's the kind of tool you buy without debate when a minor drywall job pops up.

💡 💡 Tip: Drill a small pilot hole before plunging to avoid blade wobble and get a perfectly straight start.

products.review: The double-edge design efficiently removes material with every stroke, and the sharp tip starts plunge cuts without fuss. It stays sharp through drywall, light wood, and pumpkin carving. The double-edge is clever but not essential for pure drywall work, and this saw isn't intended for heavy daily jobsite demands. For the DIYer who splits time between home renovation and seasonal projects, it adds a welcome dose of versatility at a budget-friendly price.

products.review: The Klein 725's rigid blade and beveled tip pierce drywall with little resistance, and the flat butt doubles as a box hammer. The fixed design won't fold, so storage demands a sheath or dedicated pouch slot. buyingGuide.heading: Blade Type and Material
buyingGuide.body:
Most manual drywall saws use either carbon steel or bi-metal with triple-ground teeth. Bi-metal blades (high-speed steel teeth bonded to a flexible body) resist dulling far longer than plain carbon steel, especially when you occasionally nick metal studs. A triple-ground edge tapers the tooth tips for a sharper bite with less effort—important if you’re cutting overhead.

An underappreciated detail is tooth gullet depth. Deep gullets evacuate drywall dust quickly, preventing the blade from binding on fast cuts, while shallow gullets can pack with gypsum and slow you down. For a saw that feels 'like butter,' look for at least 7 TPI with aggressive gullets.

buyingGuide.heading: Folding vs. Fixed Design and Lock Quality
buyingGuide.body:
Folding saws save space and protect the blade when thrown into a tool bag, but the hinge and lock are the weakest link. A poorly designed lock can vibrate open or, in the worst case, allow the blade to detach mid-stroke. A robust folding saw uses a lock button or collar that engages with an audible click and requires deliberate pressure to release, with no wobble when locked.

Fixed-blade saws eliminate the hinge risk entirely, offering a stiffer blade that won’t flex when you jab the tip into drywall. The trade-off is portability—you’ll need a sheath or a safe place in your toolbox so the exposed teeth don’t gouge other tools.

buyingGuide.heading: Power Source: Manual, Corded, or Cordless
buyingGuide.body:
Manual saws put you in complete control for one-off cutouts, but your arm does all the work. A corded rotary cut-out tool like a 5-amp, 30,000 RPM model tears through drywall fast, with no battery to swap—ideal for roughing in multiple boxes. The hidden cost: corded chucks often overheat and seize from gypsum dust ingress, so treat them as occasional-use tools.

Cordless cut-out tools in the 18V/20V class weigh under 3 lbs and shine in tight attic or overhead work. However, they bind more easily in 5/8-inch fire-rated board if the bit isn’t sharp, and you must already own a battery platform to keep the total price reasonable.

buyingGuide.heading: Dust Collection: When It’s Worth It
buyingGuide.body:
Traditional rotary cut-out tools spray fine gypsum dust everywhere, which lingers in the air and coats furniture. A dedicated dust-extracting cut-out saw uses a reciprocating blade inside a sealed box, capturing nearly 95% of the dust before it escapes. This is a game-changer for remodeling a lived-in home—if you can stomach the perpetual cost of proprietary replacement blades.

For occasional users, a simple dust shroud attachment on a standard rotary tool offers a compromise, but they rarely match the near-zero-dust performance of a purpose-built dustless saw.

buyingGuide.heading: Bit and Collet System: What Breaks and Costs
buyingGuide.body:
Rotary cut-out tools rely on small, high-speed bits held by a collet. If the collet doesn’t clamp evenly or loosens under vibration, the bit wobbles and snaps—often leaving the shard welded inside by friction heat. A tool-free collet is convenient for quick changes, but many require excessive hand torque to secure the bit reliably.

Beyond the initial purchase, compare consumable costs. Standard 1/8-inch drywall bits are inexpensive, but proprietary reciprocating blades on dust-free saws can double your long-term investment. If you cut dozens of boxes a week, factor the cost of replacement blades into your budget before choosing the tool.

faq.question: Why does my folding drywall saw blade keep coming loose?
faq.answer: The hinge and lock mechanism is likely under-engineered. Folding saws rely on a small detent or button that can wear quickly, especially if the pivot screw loosens from vibration. Opt for saws with a robust, audible lock-up and check the pivot screw periodically—if reviews mention blade wobble or unexpected release, pass on that model.

faq.question: Can a cordless cut‑out tool handle 5/8‑inch fire‑rated drywall?
faq.answer: Yes, but it requires a sharp bit and steady pace. Fire-rated board is denser and generates more friction, which can overheat batteries and snap bits faster than standard 1/2-inch drywall. A brushed or brushless cutoff tool with 26,000+ RPM and a fresh, high-quality carbide bit will make clean cutouts, though you may need to swap batteries more often on heavy days.

faq.question: How do I stop my rotary cut‑out bits from breaking inside the chuck?
faq.answer: Bit breakage usually stems from a collet that isn’t tightened evenly or a dull bit that catches. Seat the bit fully into the collet, then tighten by rotating the collet nut while gently wiggling the bit to ensure it centers. Pause every few cuts to clear gypsum dust from the chuck with compressed air; baked-on dust creates hotspots that seize bits. Replace bits as soon as they feel dull—a sharp bit cuts with less lateral force.

faq.question: Is the Makita dustless drywall saw worth the expensive replacement blades?
faq.answer: If you work in finished, occupied homes where dust control is non-negotiable, the blade cost is justified by the savings in cleanup and respiratory protection. For occasional users, the premium is harder to swallow. Crunch the numbers: each proprietary blade handles roughly 200–300 linear feet of drywall. If your yearly cutout workload falls far below that, the overall cost may still feel reasonable over a two-year span.
